title: Multi-Language CLI Generation
goal: Implement #393 by moving CLI package authoring toward implementation-independent definitions, off-the-shelf-first generation, generated Python and TypeScript proof slices, Docker-isolated generated-package tests, shell feasibility notes, and direct-CLI-edit guardrails.
non_goals: Move runtime primitive behavior into generated code.
Require Node, shell-specific tooling, or Docker for ordinary Python development.
Assume host repositories use GitHub or any specific external tracker.
Generate lifecycle mutation commands before read-only conformance is mature.
Build a large bespoke generator when standard schema/tooling can carry the work.

intent.outcome: Command/interface changes should be made in canonical package definitions rather than direct CLI implementation code, with Python and TypeScript generated package targets as the first portability proof.
intent.constraints: Keep the package repo-, agent-, tool-, and language-agnostic; prefer off-the-shelf generation; keep custom codegen minimal; isolate generated-package tests in Docker.
intent.latitude: Add compact contracts, generated proof fixtures, docs, checks, tests, and proof routing needed to make the roadmap operational.
intent.escalation: Stop before broad lifecycle mutation generation, broad runtime rewrites, or introducing ordinary non-Python development requirements.

execution.next_step: Implement the audit and off-the-shelf strategy milestone for #394/#395.
execution.proof: Use agentic-workspace proof --changed, focused contract tests, generated-package checks, maintainer surfaces, planning checks, and root CLI tests as selected.

invariants: Keep host-specific issue tracker behavior outside core package semantics.

intent_continuity.larger intended outcome: Generated multi-language CLI packages from implementation-independent definitions.
intent_continuity.this slice completes the larger intended: yes if all #393 child issues are satisfied and linked back to the PR

required_continuation.required follow-on for the larger intend: yes until all child issues #394-#401 are implemented or explicitly split into new issues

iterative_follow_through.what this slice enabled: A durable migration path from hand-authored CLI surfaces to generated Python and TypeScript package adapters.
iterative_follow_through.intentionally deferred: Full lifecycle mutation generation and broad shell implementation.

intent_interpretation.literal request: Create a branch, ingest issues, plan and implement the work end-to-end, commit after each milestone, create a PR, and dogfood the package with new issues for actionable improvement lanes.
intent_interpretation.inferred intended outcome: A PR that makes the generated CLI package roadmap executable rather than only planned.
intent_interpretation.chosen concrete what: Start from the active issues, add planning residue, then implement compact contracts, generation proof targets, Docker proof routing, and migration guardrails.
intent_interpretation.interpretation distance: medium because the full roadmap is large; implementation will stay bounded to thin proof slices and contract scaffolding rather than rewriting every CLI.
intent_interpretation.review guidance: Review whether each change makes direct CLI authoring less necessary without adding a new visible product maze.

execution_bounds.allowed paths: planning state/evidence/execplan, docs/reviews or docs, src/agentic_workspace/contracts, scripts/check or scripts/generate, tests, generated proof fixture directories if needed, and directly required maintainer surfaces.
execution_bounds.required validation commands: Use agentic-workspace proof --changed for each milestone; run selected tests and checks.
execution_bounds.ask-before-refactor threshold: Any rewrite of root src/agentic_workspace/cli.py or package CLI runtime behavior beyond thin generated-adapter routing.
execution_bounds.stop before touching: Runtime primitive semantics for lifecycle mutation commands; external tracker-specific core behavior.

stop_conditions.stop when: All child issues are implemented or remaining work is honestly split into new issues with acceptance evidence.
stop_conditions.escalate when boundary reached: A required generator choice would add a permanent non-Python ordinary development dependency.
stop_conditions.escalate on scope drift: The work starts turning operation contracts into a runtime workflow engine.
stop_conditions.escalate on proof failure: If selected package proof cannot pass after a bounded fix.

context_budget.live working set: #393-#401, current contracts, current generator/check scripts, this execplan.
context_budget.externalize before shift: Update this plan after every milestone and commit.
context_budget.pre-work memory pull: No broad memory pull; use compact summary and issue bodies.
context_budget.tiny resumability note: Work from #393 child order: audit/strategy, IR, Python/TS proof, Docker proof, shell/direct-edit guardrails.
context_budget.context-shift triggers: Before branch push/PR or after any failed proof.

delegated_judgment.requested outcome: Make implementation-independent multi-language CLI generation real enough to review.
delegated_judgment.hard constraints: Off-the-shelf-first; minimal custom codegen; Docker-isolated generated package tests; ordinary repo remains Python-centered; package stays agnostic.
delegated_judgment.agent may decide locally: Exact IR shape, first safe generated target, and proof lane details.
delegated_judgment.escalate when: The work would require committing to a large custom generator or changing runtime primitive ownership.

immediate_next_action: Add compact audit and off-the-shelf strategy artifacts for #394/#395, then validate and commit milestone 1.
invariants: Do not create ordinary non-Python development requirements.
Keep host-specific workflow out of core package definitions.
contract_decisions_to_freeze: Generated adapters own command/interface wiring; runtime primitives own behavior.
open_questions_to_close: Which off-the-shelf tools should be preferred for schema/model generation?
Is a new command-package IR necessary or can it be derived from existing contracts?
What is the smallest Python/TypeScript generated package proof?
required_tools: Docker only for the generated-package proof lane.
completion_criteria: #394-#401 are either closed with implementation evidence or split into new follow-up issues.
The branch contains a PR-ready implementation of the roadmap's first generated package path.

execution_run.what happened: Created branch, ingested issue bodies, and initialized checked-in planning.

finished_run_review.config compliance: package dogfooding started with start, summary, ownership, and checked-in planning
finished_run_review.misinterpretation risk: medium; keep each milestone bounded and evidence-backed

intent_satisfaction.original intent: Create branch, ingest issues, plan, implement end-to-end, commit after each milestone, create PR, and create new issues for actionable dogfooding improvements.

system_intent_alignment.relevant system intent: Do not let this repo's current language, tooling, structure, or agent preferences become hidden universal product assumptions.
system_intent_alignment.slice shaping bias: Implementation-independent definitions first; generated targets as proof, not product sprawl.
system_intent_alignment.broader-lane validation question: Does this make generated CLI work easier to enter, verify, and trust without turning code generation into a new visible framework?

drift_log: 2026-04-27: Created active plan from #393-#401 after branch creation.
